coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection based on two-dimensional leaf-like structure CDs@ZIF-L
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Mingkun
Liu, Guangyang
Tan, Qiyue
Zhao, Chenxi
Chen, Ge
Zhai, Rongqi
Hua, Yuwei
Huang, Xiaodong
Wang, Jing
Xu, Donghui
coredata.dc:description: A novel fluorescent composite (CDs@ZIF-L) was synthesized for the selective detection of Fe3+ based on the encapsulation of Carbon dots (CDs) into Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF) with a two-dimensional leaf-like structure. The physicochemical structure, surface morphology, and composition of the adsorbent were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and thermogravimetric analysis. The results showed that unlike the conventional three-dimensional rhombic structure (ZIF-8), the synthesized two-dimensional leaf-like nanosheets (ZIF-L) not only maintained the structural integrity but also had a permeable pore structure. Importantly, at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the composite's had significant fluorescence emission at 445 nm and was effectively quenched in the presence of Fe3+, which can be used as an effective fluorescent scaling probe. Under the optimal conditions, CDs@ZIF-L was used as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ with good linearity in the range of 0.02–2 mM (R2 = 0.9948) and a low detection limit of 0.891 μM. The fluorescent probe can also be used for the determination of Fe3+ in fruit samples with good recovery and accuracy, showing great potential for applications in environmental monitoring and agricultural pollutant control. It has good recovery and accuracy and shows great potential for environmental monitoring and agricultural pollutant control.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks
Fluorescence sensing
Iron ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
Optical and structural properties of CDs@ZIF-L
Effect of solution concentration on fluorescence sensing
Effect of solution pH on fluorescence sensing
Effect of coexisting ion concentration on fluorescence sensing
Effect of reaction time on fluorescence sensing
Interaction mechanism of CDs@ZIF-L with Fe3+ ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthetic principle of CDs@ZIF-L and the mechanism of Fe3+ detection.
(a) SEM images of CDs@ZIF-L and (b) TEM images of ZIF-L, (c) CDs@ZIF-L (insets are HR-TEM images of lattice stripes of CDs@ZIF-L). EDS (d) and elemental mapping (e-j) of CDs@ZIF-L.
(a) XRD and (b) FTIR patterns of the different samples.
(a) TGA curves and (b) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of different samples.

(a) Fluorescence spectra plots of Fe3+ (0–10 mM) added at different concentrations under 350 nm excitation. The inset shows the corresponding color change of CDs@ZIF-L after the addition of Fe3+. (b) Linear relationship between the relative fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-L and the concentration of Fe3+.
Content of each element in ZIF-L and CDs@ZIF-L composites.
ZIF-L and CDs@ZIF-L gas adsorption data of Composites.
CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent probes for Fe3+ detection in real samples.
Comparison of common materials and the present method in the detection of Fe3+.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection based on two-dimensional leaf-like structure CDs@ZIF-L

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Vegetables and Flowers, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Vegetables Quality and Safety Control, Laboratory of Quality& Safety Risk Assessment for vegetable Products, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of China, Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Vegetables Quality and Safety Control
Laboratory of Quality& Safety Risk Assessment for vegetable Products
Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Vegetables and Flowers, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Vegetables Quality and Safety Control, Laboratory of Quality& Safety Risk Assessment for vegetable Products, Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs of China, Beijing, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology for Agro-Products, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Agro-Product Quality and Safety, Ministry of Agriculture Beijing, 100081 Beijing, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology for Agro-Products
Key Laboratory of Agro-Product Quality and Safety

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Quality Standard and Testing Technology for Agro-Products, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Key Laboratory of Agro-Product Quality and Safety, Ministry of Agriculture Beijing, 100081 Beijing, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A two-dimensional leaf-like nanosheet structure of CDs@ZIF-L was constructed to exploit the two-dimensional structure to expose more adsorption sites that could synergistically improve the sensitivity of detection in fruit samples.

A novel fluorescent composite (CDs@ZIF-L) was synthesized for the selective detection of Fe3+ based on the encapsulation of Carbon dots (CDs) into Zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF) with a two-dimensional leaf-like structure. The physicochemical structure, surface morphology, and composition of the adsorbent were characterized by scanning electron microscopy, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and thermogravimetric analysis. The results showed that unlike the conventional three-dimensional rhombic structure (ZIF-8), the synthesized two-dimensional leaf-like nanosheets (ZIF-L) not only maintained the structural integrity but also had a permeable pore structure. Importantly, at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, the composite's had significant fluorescence emission at 445 nm and was effectively quenched in the presence of Fe3+, which can be used as an effective fluorescent scaling probe. Under the optimal conditions, CDs@ZIF-L was used as a fluorescent probe to detect Fe3+ with good linearity in the range of 0.02–2 mM (R2 = 0.9948) and a low detection limit of 0.891 μM. The fluorescent probe can also be used for the determination of Fe3+ in fruit samples with good recovery and accuracy, showing great potential for applications in environmental monitoring and agricultural pollutant control. It has good recovery and accuracy and shows great potential for environmental monitoring and agricultural pollutant control.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron ions (Fe3+) are essential transition metal ions in biological systems, and deficiency or excess of iron can lead to certain diseases such as anemia, heart failure, diabetes, and cancer [1–3]. In addition, Fe3+ has become a major pollutant of environmental degradation due to the misuse of metallic materials in chemical production, posing a serious threat to the environment and health [4,5]. Although current detection techniques, including ion chromatography or inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, can be used for the determination of Fe3+, their further practical application is greatly limited by the drawbacks of expensive instruments, long experimental cycles, and complicated operation [6]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a reliable and sensitive fluorescent probe for Fe3+ identification sensing.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a novel nanoluminescent material with quantum dimensions, have attracted much attention due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, and high biocompatibility [7–13]. The abundant surface sites and functional groups, easily modified structures, and excellent electron transfer properties of CDs make them good electron donors and acceptors. However, CDs tend to aggregate during electron transfer leading to fluorescence bursts [14–16]. Therefore, modulating the fluorescence properties of composites by introducing CDs as guest materials into different kinds of host matrices to cause coupling effects and interactions between their hosts and guests is an effective way to solve the above problems.

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are composites composed of metal ions and organic ligands through coordination bonding, with large specific surface area and tunable pore size structure, which have received a lot of attention in the fields of gas separation [17], catalysis [18], and organic pollutant adsorption [19], especially the closure of pore channels will greatly reduce the aggregation and migration of substances, which makes MOFs a good dispersion and protection of CDs choice [20–24]. In recent years, researchers have mostly realized the preparation of composites and developed their multiple applications by encapsulating CDs within ZIF-L during the crystallization of nanoparticles, which traditionally have three-dimensional dimensions thanks largely to the high dispersion of exposed sites in nanoparticles [25–27], while two-dimensionality also increases the outer surface area and thus exposes more adsorption sites [28–30]. Therefore, the development of 2D ZIF-L with the same topology as ZIF-8 and its application in contaminant recognition and removal has great potential.

In this study, the composites were prepared by encapsulating water-soluble fluorescent CDs in the pores of ZIF-L by in situ synthesis at room temperature. Unlike the conventional three-dimensional rhombic structure (ZIF-8), the synthesized two-dimensional leaf-like nanosheets (CDs@ZIF-L) maintain the structural integrity and have a permeable pore structure (Fig. 1 ), which can not only reduce the aggregation and migration of substances and thus enhance the fluorescence stability of CDs, but also expose more adsorption sites in the two-dimensional structure to synergistically improve the sensitivity of the reaction. The fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-L was effectively quenched in the presence of Fe3+, and the fluorescence of CDs@ZIF-L was significantly emitted at 445 nm under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm. Therefore, the fluorescence sensor constructed based on the composites can be used to detect Fe3+ in real samples, which shows great potential for applications in environmental monitoring and agricultural pollutant control.

Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (Zn(NO3)2·6H2O) and 2-methylimidazole (2-MeIM) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Cysteine (Cys) and Glucose (Glu) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). The aqueous solution of Ca2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Na2+, Mg2+, was prepared from nitrate salts. Carbon dots (CDs) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich Chemicals Co. All reagents used were of analytical grade and were used without any further purification.

The morphological structures of the synthesized ZIF-L and CDs@ZIF-L were measured using a scanning electron microscope (SEM, SM-6300, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan). X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded with a D8 Advance X-ray powder diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). The surface chemical composition and functional groups of all materials were confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet iS10, Thermo Scientific, USA), respectively. Thermogravimetric analysis was performed on a thermogravimetric analyzer (DSC823 Thermogravimetric Analyzer/SDTA85/Eastern Switzerland). The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area and porosity of the materials were determined on an ASAP 2020 analyzer (Micromeritics Instrument Corp, Norcross, GA, USA) using N2 adsorption–desorption isotherm experiments. All fluorescence measurements were carried out using a Perkin Elmer LS45 (Massachusetts, US) fluorescence spectrophotometer. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu UV2550 spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan).

Two-dimensional leaf-like structured nanosheets (ZIF-L) were synthesized by a simple precipitation method [31]. First, 1.6625 g of 2-methylimidazole was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water to form solution A and 0.7732 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water solution to form solution B. The above solution A was rapidly added to solution B, sonicated for 3 min, and then stirred vigorously for 30 min, followed by the aging of the resulting solution at room temperature for 24. The resulting solution was then aged at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the samples obtained were dried overnight at 60 °C by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 3 min) and washed three times with alternating ethanol/deionized water to obtain 2D ZIF-L powder.

The synthesis of CDs@ZIF-L was carried out according to the literature and with slight modifications [32,33]. First, 0.5 mL of CDs (3 mg/mL) was added to 50 mL of solution in which 1.6625 g of 2-methylimidazole was dissolved and magnetically stirred for 30 min at room temperature to form a homogeneous solution A. 0.7732 g of Zn(NO3)2·6H2O was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water solution to form solution B. The above solution A was rapidly added to solution B, sonicated for 3 min and then the resulting solution was magnetically stirred at 37 °C for 48 h. Finally, the sample was washed three times by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 3 min) with ethanol and dried under vacuum to obtain a white CDs@ZIF-L powder.

Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ was performed by fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. First, 0.3 mL of CDs@ZIF-L (0.05 mg/mL) and 0.3 mL of PBS buffer solution (20 mM) were taken and reacted for 15 min, then 0.45 mL of different concentrations of Fe3+ was added and shaken vigorously for 30 min under light-proof conditions then transferred to a quartz cell. A series of fluorescence measurement experiments were performed at a given excitation wavelength of 350 nm and excitation and emission slit widths of 3 nm, and each experiment was repeated three times. A calibration curve for Fe3+ detection was established based on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-L in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+. Finally, the LOD based on the CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent probe was calculated using the equation 3S/N. Under optimal conditions, the same initial concentrations of interfering ions (including Ca2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Glu, Cys, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Na+, and Mg2+) were added to the mixture to assess the selectivity of CDs@ZIF-L for Fe3+ selectivity and anti-interference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe3+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: All samples (apples and oranges) were obtained from local supermarkets. First, all samples were washed with tap water and ultrapure water and thoroughly homogenized with a commercial blender. Fifteen mL of deionized water was added to the homogenate, vortexed for 5 min to extract the analytes, and then the supernatant was extracted by centrifugation (8000 rpm, 10 min) and diluted with deionized water. Then, the liquid was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane to remove the residual particulate matter, and different concentrations of Fe3+ were added to the obtained supernatant and detected using a CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent probe. The Fe3+ content in the actual sample was determined from the obtained working curve, and the experiment was repeated three times under the same conditions to obtain the relative standard deviation and calculate the recovery.

The morphology and structure of the synthesized ZIF-L and CDs@ZIF-L were initially observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The disordered stacked blade-like structures with relatively uniform size and length of about 4.5 μm can be obtained from the SEM and TEM images of Fig. 2
a-b, indicating the successful preparation of 2D ZIF-L. Meanwhile, carbon dots with a particle size of about 5 nm distributed in CDs@ZIF-L can be observed in Fig. 2 c, and the high-resolution transmission microscopy (HR-TEM) images indicate that the lattice spacing of carbon dots is 0.23 nm in agreement with that of ZIF-L, which indicates that CDs have been successfully encapsulated in the cavities of ZIF-L. In addition, the surface composition of the material was analyzed by energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). As shown in Fig. 2 d, the material consists of four elements, C, N, O, and Zn, and the element mapping images (Fig. 2 e-j) show that the corresponding elements are uniformly distributed on the composites, and the above results indicate the successful modification of the CDs into the cavities of ZIF-L with a two-dimensional leaf-like structure by the encapsulation strategy.

The X-ray diffraction pattern (Fig. 3
a) shows that both ZIF-L and CDs@ZIF-L have a typical leaf-like structure consistent with previous ZIF-L in the literature [34], and by comparison with the standard card for ZIF-8 (CCDC 602542) it was found that the most intense peak in the XRD pattern of 2D ZIF-L occurs in the (222) plane at 18.1° instead of the 7.4° of the (020) plane [35]. Moreover, unlike ZIF-8, the change in the crystal structure of ZIF-L resulted in more secondary intense peaks (11.1°, 15.2°, 17.3°), showing that the two-dimensional leafy ZIF material has a more complex crystal structure than the porous ZIF material, which does not show significant carbon peaks in the figure due to the low carbon content, but is characterized by 7.4° (011), 10.4°(002), 7.4° (002), 10.4° (112), and sharp diffraction peaks at 12.8° (222) can prove that the obtained materials are highly crystalline and impurity-free 2D CDs@ZIF-L.

The surface chemical structure of CDs@ZIF-L was further characterized by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), and it is obvious from Fig. 3 b that the synthesized CDs@ZIF-L has a characteristic absorption band similar to that of ZIF-L. The absorption peaks at 3134 cm−1 and 2927 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration peaks of the CH bond on the imidazole ring and the methyl group, respectively. The peaks at 1518 cm−1 and 1146 cm−1 belonged to the stretching vibration peaks of the CN and CN bonds, demonstrating the involvement of 2-methylimidazole in the synthesis of the ZIF-L structure. Moreover, the absorption peaks at 3420 cm−1, 2480 cm−1, and 1960 cm−1 appearing in the CDs@ZIF-L composites correspond to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of OH, CO, and CC = C bonds, respectively [28]. The result further suggests that CDs are successfully encapsulated within the cavity of ZIF-L.

To further determine the composition of the samples, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed. As shown in Fig. S1 and Table 1 , XPS of different samples in the range of 0–1300 eV revealed the presence of Zn, C, O, and N elements ( Fig. S2
a). The high-resolution XPS spectra more clearly identified the binding energy peaks of C 1 s (282.6 eV), O 1 s (529.1 eV), N 1 s (397.6 eV), and Zn 2p (1005.5 eV). Furthermore, in the photoelectron spectrum of the C 1 s core energy level, peaks of 284.5, 285.8, and 288.5 eV are attributed to the presence of CC, C sp3, and CN, respectively ( Fig. S2
b) [36], and these carbon peaks originate from the 2-methylimidazole and carbon dots in the composite. In the photoelectron spectrum of the N 1 s core energy level, the peaks at 399.0, 401.0, and 406.5 can be attributed to CNC, NH, and NO ( Fig. S3
c) [37], while the characteristic absorption peak of O 1 s around 533 eV in the photoelectron spectrum is generated through the action of CO at 532.0 eV and CO at 533.5 eV ( Fig. S4
d) [38]. These results further demonstrate the successful preparation of CDs@ZIF-L.

The thermal stability of the composites was further assessed by TGA analysis, where CDs@ZIF-L was heated in N2 from room temperature to 800 °C. The first mass loss of CDs@ZIF-L occurred between 200 and 300 °C (Fig. 4
a), with a loss of about 15 % of the total mass, which can be attributed to the removal of residual solvent and the decomposition of the amide groups in CDs. The mass loss phase from 500 to 650 °C can be attributed to the collapse of the framework, which suggests that the thermal stability of CDs@ZIF-L can reach above 600 °C, which is consistent with previous reports [32]. The above analysis can indicate that CDs@ZIF-L has good thermal stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural properties of CDs@ZIF-L
ce:section.ce:para:
The synthesized composites were homogeneously dispersed in an aqueous solution and the optical properties of CDs@ZIF-L were tested by UV–vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence emission spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S3
a, the UV–vis spectra of both ZIF-L and CDs@ZIF-L showed two unique sharp absorption peaks near 227 and 358 nm, which can be attributed to the action of the organic ligand 2-methylimidazole in ZIF-L [40]. Meanwhile, the CDs@ZIF-L composites show a broad absorption band at 300 and 400 nm, a phenomenon that may arise from the leaping action of the CO bond at the carbon site [41]. Moreover, as shown in the inset in Fig. S3
b, CDs@ZIF-L appear milky white in natural light, and an intense blue fluorescence emission peak can be observed at 445 nm at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. However, there is no obvious emission peak for pure ZIF-L. These results suggest that the blue fluorescence of the composite comes from CDs rather than ZIF-L, which is caused by the fluorescence properties of CDs being retained in the composite.

To further evaluate the feasibility of CDs@ZIF-L as a fluorescent probe in its real samples, the fluorescence and structural stability of the composite was investigated. When CDs@ZIF-L was dispersed in an aqueous solution and stored for different times, it was found that the fluorescence intensity did not change severely ( Fig. S4
a). In addition, uniform dispersion of CDs@ZIF-L in an aqueous solution was carried out at room temperature for 6, 12, and 24 h. The results are shown in Fig. S4
b. By comparing the XRD diffraction peaks of the treated composites with those of the initial composites, it was found that there was no change in the material structure, and the above results indicate that CDs@ZIF-L has good fluorescence and structural stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution concentration on fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The concentration of CDs@ZIF-L solution is very important for fluorescence detection, too large a concentration of iron ions can not be completely captured, and too small a concentration can not be fully combined with iron ion solution, therefore, different concentrations of CDs@ZIF-L (0.01, 0.02, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15 and 0.20 mg/mL) were selected to obtain the optimal solution concentration to ensure the experimental accuracy. The experimental results are shown in Fig. S5
a, where the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased rapidly as the solution concentration increased from 0.01 to 0.05 mg/mL, which may be due to the increase in the composite material providing more adsorption sites and increasing the contact efficiency with ferric ions [42]. However, with further increase in solution concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency showed a gradual decrease because the initial iron ion concentration remained constant and most of the adsorption sites on the surface of the composites were completely occupied during the adsorption process, resulting in a gradual decrease in the quenching efficiency. Therefore, 0.05 mg/mL was chosen as the optimal concentration of CDs@ZIF-L solution in all subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of solution pH on fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
Since some of the fluorescent probes can only be detected under specific acid-base conditions, the surface state of CDs@ZIF-L composites will vary under different pH conditions. Therefore, pH not only affects the fluorescence intensity and stability of the original CDs@ZIF-L but also affects the subsequent fluorescence burst of Fe3+. By studying the effect on the CDs@ZIF-L- Fe3+ system in the pH range of 2–12. The experimental results are shown in Fig. S5
b. In the pH range of 2–6, the quenching efficiency of CDs@ZIF-L gradually increased with the pH value of the system, and the quenching efficiency of the system reached the maximum when the pH value was 6; when the pH value was in the range of 6–8, the fluorescence quenching efficiency decreased from 40 % to 19 %, showing an obvious decreasing trend, while when the pH value was between 6.6 and 7.4, the bursting efficiency remained almost at 15 %, indicating that the alkaline conditions were not favorable for the CDs@ZIF-L- Fe3+ system. The above results indicate that the sensing system has good detection in a weakly acidic environment, and a pH value of 6 is most suitable for the detection of Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of coexisting ion concentration on fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
Generally speaking, the solubility of analytes in aqueous solutions is influenced by the ionic strength of the solutions, therefore, different concentrations of PBS (10, 20, 50, 80, and 100 mM) were chosen to investigate the effect of different concentrations of PBS buffer solutions on the fluorescent probes. As shown in Fig. S5
c, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of CDs@ZIF-L composites gradually increased from 46 % to 57 % in the ion concentration range of 10–20 mM, and the quenching efficiency of the reaction system reached the maximum when the ion concentration was 20 mM. However, with the further increase of ion concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency showed a clear decreasing trend, and the burst quenching efficiency was kept floating around 52 % in the range of 50–100 mM. It is clear from the above data that the fluorescence quenching efficiency is highest at the coexisting ion concentration of 20 mM. Therefore, 20 mM PBS was chosen as the optimal ion concentration in the subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of reaction time on fluorescence sensing
ce:section.ce:para:
The reaction time is also an important parameter of the fluorescent probe, and the effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity was investigated by measuring the fluorescence intensity at room temperature for 5, 10, 15, 20, 30, 45, and 60 min. The results are shown in Fig. S5
d. Due to the high concentration difference of solution concentration and the presence of adsorption sites on the surface of CDs@ZIF-L composite, the fluorescence quenching efficiency increased rapidly in the range of 5–30 min and reached 92 % at 30 min, while the long rate of fluorescence quenching efficiency slowed down significantly and tended to equilibrate with the increasing time. This phenomenon can be attributed to the large specific surface area of CDs@ZIF-L composites with two-dimensional leaf-like lamellae structure, which makes the Fe3+ ions adsorption and diffusion in the pore size of CDs@ZIF-L with faster kinetic performance, on the one hand, and the decrease in the number of adsorption sites on the surface of the composites, on the other hand. In summary, 30 min can be considered as the optimal reaction time.

The effect of Fe3+ on the fluorescence spectra of CDs@ZIF-L was measured and recorded under optimal conditions. As shown in Fig. 5
a, the fluorescence intensity of CDs@ZIF-L at 445 nm gradually decreased with increasing Fe3+ concentration, which was caused by the quenching effect of Fe3+ on the carbon dots in CDs@ZIF-L. In addition, the relative fluorescence intensity of Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0.02–2 mM (Fig. 5 b) showed a good linear relationship with the concentration (R2 = 0.9948), and the relative fluorescence intensity (F0/F) was expressed by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1)
F 0
/F = 1 + Ksv
Fe
3 +
where [Fe3+] is the concentration of Fe3+, Ksv is the quenching constant, and F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively. To evaluate the sensitivity of the fluorescent probe, the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated by statistical treatment of the calibration curve according to the equation LOD = 3S/K. The detection limit based on CDs@ZIF-L for Fe3+ detection was 0.891 μM (S/N = 3).

In practical applications, high selectivity has also been used as an important factor in the evaluation of CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent probes. Interference studies were carried out under optimal experimental conditions using some common ions such as Ca2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Glu, Cys, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Na+ and Mg2+. As shown in Fig. S6 , by comparing the fluorescence intensity of the composites at 445 nm, it was found that the effect on the fluorescence intensity of all ions except Fe3+ was minimal. It is noteworthy that Fe3+ almost completely quenched the fluorescence of the composites. As a comparison, the anti-interference ability of CDs@ZIF-L was further investigated by mixing Fe3+ with various interfering ions, and Fig. S6 shows that Fe3+ still has the same degree of fluorescence quenching in coexistence with other interfering ions, indicating that the prepared CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent probes have good selectivity for Fe3+.

To evaluate the accuracy and applicability of the CDs@ZIF-l-based fluorescent probe in practical applications, spiking and recovery experiments were conducted in apple and orange samples, respectively. To verify the practicality of the assay. As shown in Table 3 , the spiked recoveries ranged from 102.6 to 103.0 % for apple samples and 99.156–100.2 % for orange samples, confirming that the CDs@ZIF-L fluorescent probe was used for the detection of Fe3+ in fruits without significant interference. The above results indicated that CDs@ZIF-L has high accuracy and reliability for the determination of Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction mechanism of CDs@ZIF-L with Fe3+ ions
ce:section.ce:para:
The interaction mechanism of Fe3+ ions on CDs@ZIF-L was analyzed from the following aspects, respectively. First, the adsorption accumulation effect of the composite on Fe3+ ions was investigated. The addition of Fe3+ to CDs@ZIF-L produced a yellow precipitate and a colorless supernatant ( Fig. S7
a right) by centrifuging the blue solution ( Fig. S7
a left), indicating that the larger specific surface area of ZIF-L in the fluorescent probe improves the adsorption capacity of the sensing system can significantly adsorb Fe3+ ions. Fe3+ ions can form complexes with the imidazole ring in the ZIF-L structure, thus improving the sensitivity of the detection [15,43]. Secondly, the fluorescence burst effect between CDs@ZIF-L composites and Fe3+ ions was investigated. Fig. S7
a shows a decrease in absorbance at 345 nm due to the presence of Fe3+, which is in perfect agreement with the excitation wavelength of the composite, and a new adsorption peak in the 325–350 nm range of the material, indicating that Fe3+ can react with the carboxyl or amino groups on the surface of CDs to form UV-active complexes can attenuate the fluorescence intensity on the surface of CDs.

Second, since there is almost no absorption peak in the UV–vis spectrum of Fe3+ ions, it can be suggested that the fluorescence burst effect between CDs@ZIF-L composites and Fe3+ ions is not caused by fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). Therefore, the fluorescence burst mechanism of Fe3+ ions on CDs@ZIF-L composites can be considered as similar to the burst mechanism on CDs, which can be divided into two main parts as follows. In the first part, Fe3+ ions are adsorbed in the pores of ZIF-L and form a complex through the chemical replacement of Zn2+ ions with Fe3+ ions. To investigate the effect of the structure of the formed intermediate composite on its fluorescence quenching, by analyzing the XRD patterns of the composite before and after the Fe3+ ion treatment, as shown in Fig. S7
b, the structure of the composite largely disappeared after the Fe3+ ion treatment with the characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11.1° and 2θ = 15.2°, 7.4°(011), 10.4° (002) The intensity of the peaks at 7.4°(002), 10.4°(112) and 12.8°(222) weakened to different degrees, and this phenomenon can be attributed to the synergistic accumulation of CDs@ZIF-L in Fe3+ solution for Fe3+ ions due to the higher specific surface area of ZIF-L present in the composite structure. At the same time, the exchange of Fe3+ and Zn2+ cations to form new amorphous structures led to structural changes of CDs@ZIF-L [44]. In the second part, Fe3+ ions can form complexes with carboxyl or amino functional groups on the surface of CDs when Fe3+ ions diffuse into CDs through adsorption in the ZIF-L pores. To gain insight into the sensing mechanism, the fluorescence burst of CDs@ZIF-L with Fe3+ was further discussed using the FTIR technique, and the comparison of FTIR spectra before and after the addition of Fe3+ ions to the CDs@ZIF-L suspension revealed that the characteristic absorption peaks at 2480 and 3134 cm−1 disappeared ( Fig. S7
c), and the stretching vibrational bands at 1518 cm−1 and 1146 cm−1 were shifted to 1550 cm−1 and 1250 cm−1, respectively, and the intensities of other characteristic adsorption peaks were also significantly weakened, a phenomenon attributable to the substitution of Fe3+ for Zn2+ on the surface of CDs@ZIF-L with oxygen group coordination, which in turn led to the transfer of electrons from the photoexcited CDs to Fe3+ ions, resulting in a great burst in the fluorescence intensity of the composite [45,46].

Finally, to further demonstrate that the CDs@ZIF-L composite has a stronger sensing ability than CDs, a suspension of the composite with the same fluorescence intensity and a solution of CDs were added with Fe3+ ions in the same amount (5 mM). The results are shown in Fig. S7
d. The fluorescence burst intensity of the composites was much greater than that of CDs due to the adsorption of ZIF-L. The experimental results indicate that the encapsulation of CDs in ZIF-L played an important role in improving the sensing ability.

As shown in Table 4 , the performance differences between the existing fluorescence sensing systems and the present method were compared, and the indirect detection could provide an innovative method for effective detection. After the comparison of LOD, linear range, and R2, it was observed that the fluorescence sensor prepared based on CDs@ZIF-L showed good sensitivity and selectivity in the detection of Fe3+ in fruit samples. Although there are a large number of sensing methods for the direct or indirect detection of Fe3+, most of them are limited to specific applications in aqueous environments, while research on the application in samples such as fruits is still lacking, and this method will play a greater role in the fluorescence detection in agricultural samples.

In the present study, we constructed a novel fluorescent probe by encapsulating water-soluble fluorescent CDs in the pores of ZIF-L at room temperature. Unlike the conventional three-dimensional rhombic structure (ZIF-8), the synthesized nanosheets with two-dimensional leaf-like nanosheets (ZIF-L) can not only maintain the structural integrity with a permeable pore structure but also improve the sensitivity of detection by the strong adsorption ability of MOFs in terms of application performance. Importantly, the CDs@ZIF-L composites have good selectivity and anti-interference in an aqueous solution for the quantitative detection of Fe3+ with good linearity in the range of 0.02–2 mM (R2 = 0.9948) and LOD of 0.891 μM. CDs@ZIF-L was applied as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ in fruit samples with the recoveries were 99.156–103.0 %, indicating that the method is applicable and shows great potential for application in environmental monitoring and agricultural.

Mingkun Gao: Validation, Data curation. Guangyang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Qiyue Tan: Validation. Chenxi Zhao: Validation. Ge Chen: Data curation. Rongqi Zhai: Validation, Visualization, Investigation. Yuwei Hua: Resources, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Xiaodong Huang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Jing Wang: Data curation. Donghui Xu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Funding: this work was supported by the National Key Research Development Program of China [grant number 2020YFD1000300]; the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number 31872947] and China Agriculture Research System of MOF and MARA (CARS-23-E03).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107868.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
